text: The surgeon positions the robotic ports in a low transverse orientation, with the second arm placed in a suprapubic location. A 12mm port is used for the extraction incision. The mesentery is being dissected, ensuring grossly negative margins and removing a rim of the mesentery.

The surgeon positions the ports in a low transverse orientation, with arm number two located suprapubically, preparing to upsize it for the extraction incision. The mesentery is being dissected to ensure grossly negative margins, with a rim of the mesentery being taken.
In firefly mode, the surgeon uses a stapler to divide the margins of the small bowel, with enhanced visualization of tissue perfusion.
In firefly mode, the surgeon uses a stapler to divide the margins of the small bowel, with enhanced visualization of tissue perfusion.

The surgeon aligns the antiparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis, creating enterotomies in the bowel. A stay suture is placed on the near side to assist in positioning the bowel onto the vascular load stapler, which is introduced through the suprapubic port.

The surgeon aligns the antiparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis and creates enterotomies. A stay suture is placed on the near side to assist in positioning the bowel onto the vascular load stapler, which is introduced through the suprapubic port.

The surgeon removes the stay suture and begins to close the common channel using a 3-0 V-Loc suture. Starting at the near corner, the surgeon performs an over-and-over stitch, followed by imbrication, using the same suture throughout the process.

The surgeon cuts the stay suture and begins to sew the common channel closed using a 3-0 V-Loc suture. Starting at the near corner, the surgeon performs an over-and-over stitch, then imbricates the tissue while moving back down, all with the same suture.
The surgeon performs an over-and-over stitch technique, extending the suture to the far corner of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon performs an over-and-over stitch, extending the suture to the far corner of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon begins to imbricate the bowel, sewing back down the previously aligned antiparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a continuous suture technique.

The surgeon begins to imbricate back down the anastomosis, using a single-layer canal suture technique to ensure no exposed puckering mucosa. The suture is carefully placed to cover the near corner, creating a consistent two-layer closure.
The surgeon ties a knot to secure the anastomosis, covering it with omentum and burying it beneath the transverse colon. The specimen is extracted through the suprapubic 10mm port site.
The surgeon covers the anastomosis with omentum, burying it beneath the transverse colon. The specimen is extracted through the suprapubic port site.
